perturbation.original_text: Any confidential information exchanged between HRM and the PNS “shall only be used for the pur poses of facilitating the business relationship between the Parties” and once the agreement terminates each party must “promptly return or destroy” all confidential information received from the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Any confidential information exchanged between HRM and the PNS “shall only be used for the pur poses of facilitating the business relationship between the Parties”.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the requirement to "promptly return or destroy" confidential information upon termination, the agreement potentially violates privacy laws that mandate the secure disposal of sensitive data after its intended use has ended.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information International Disclosure Protection Act, SN 2017, c 3, s 7
perturbation.law_explanation: The Act speaks to the protection of personal information, and while this is a non-disclosure agreement, failing to specify the destruction of confidential information upon termination of the agreement could be construed as non-compliance with principles of secure data handling and data minimization, especially if that confidential information includes personal data. Section 7 relates to the handling of records of personal information.

perturbation.justification: The original contract requires the return or destruction of confidential information upon termination, aligning with data protection principles. By omitting this requirement in the changed text, the agreement creates a legal loophole regarding the secure disposal of sensitive data, potentially conflicting with privacy laws mandating data minimization, as mentioned in the Law Explanation: "failing to specify the destruction of confidential information upon termination of the agreement could be construed as non-compliance with principles of secure data handling and data minimization, especially if that confidential information includes personal data." While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly applicable, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight the importance of secure data handling which the omission undermines, leading to a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by such Party, its employees, agents, officials, representatives or consultants acting within the scope of their engagement or employment.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by such Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'its employees, agents, officials, representatives or consultants acting within the scope of their engagement or employment,' the modified agreement does not explicitly hold the parties responsible for the actions of their representatives. This weakens the protection of confidential information and creates a contradiction with general principles of agency law, where a principal is responsible for the acts of their agents.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under common law principles, a principal (the Province of Nova Scotia or Halifax Regional Municipality) is generally liable for the actions of its agents (employees, consultants, etc.) when those agents act within the scope of their authority. Removing this explicit statement of responsibility contradicts these established legal principles, leaving room for argument as to who is liable in case of a breach by an agent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: agency | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you agency Agency law is a common law doctrine controlling relationships between agents and principals . A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party . The two forms of actual authority include: Express authority : An agent has express authority to take any actions requested by the principal as well as authority to take any actions inherently necessary to accomplish those requests. Implied authority : An agent has implied authority to take any action the principal’s conduct indicates the agent should do. Furthermore, failure by the principal to object to an agent’s prior actions may create implied authority to repeat those actions in the future. An agent does not have implied authority to do anything expressly prohibited by the principal. In addition to actual authority, a principal may be bound by the actions of an agent if apparent authority existed. Apparent authority : An agent has apparent authority when, despite no existing authority, a third party reasonably infers that someone is authorized to act on the principal’s behalf due to the conduct of the principal. Unlike actual authority, a principal can be bound by an agent’s act made with apparent authority even if they explicitly stated that the agent could not do that act. Furthermore, a person possessing a widely recognized title like Hiring Director has apparent authority to accomplish anything a reasonable person would believe that title entails. An agent acting with apparent authority is known as an ostensible agent . Additionally, principals can be held liable for the torts of their agents under the doctrine of vicarious liability . A principal is always liable for torts committed while the agent completes their official responsibilities. For torts occurring outside of official duties , the liability of the principal depends on whether the agent’s tort occurred during a frolic or a detour. A principal is liable for the “detours” of their agent but not for the “frolics.” The primary considerations many courts use to determine if an act was a frolic or a detour include how much control the principal exerts over the agent’s actions and who economically benefits from the agent’s actions. See: Pyne v. Witmer, 129 Ill. 2d 351 (1989) . All states Alabama Alaska Arizona Arkansas California Colorado Connecticut Delaware District of Columbia Florida Georgia Hawaii Idaho Illinois Indiana Iowa Kansas Kentucky Louisiana Maine Maryland Massachusetts Michigan Minnesota Mississippi Missouri Montana Nebraska Nevada New Hampshire New Jersey New Mexico New York North Carolina North Dakota Ohio Oklahoma Oregon Pennsylvania Rhode Island South Carolina South Dakota Tennessee Texas Utah Vermont Virginia Washington West Virginia Wisconsin Wyoming Select your State Alabama Alaska Arizona Arkansas California Colorado Connecticut Delaware District of Columbia Florida Georgia Hawaii Idaho Illinois Indiana Iowa Kansas Kentucky Louisiana Maine Maryland Massachusetts Michigan Minnesota Mississippi Missouri Montana Nebraska Nevada New Hampshire New Jersey New Mexico New York North Carolina North Dakota Ohio Oklahoma Oregon Pennsylvania Rhode Island South Carolina South Dakota Tennessee Texas Utah Vermont Virginia Washington West Virginia Wisconsin Wyoming Search Popular forms Show Last Will and Testament Power of Attorney Promissory Note LLC Operating Agreement Living Will Rental Lease Agreement Non-Disclosure Agreement Agency Law and Legal Definition Agency law is concerned with any "principal"-"agent" relationship; a relationship in which one person has legal authority to act for another. The relationships generally associated with agency law include guardian-ward, executor or administrator-decedent, and employer-employee. Agency is an agreement, express , or implied, by which one of the parties, called the principal, entrusts to the other, called the agent, the management of some business; to be transacted in his name, or on his account, and by which the agent assumes to do the business and to render an account of it. As a general rule, whatever a man may do by himself, except by virtue of a delegated authority, he may do by an agent. When the agency is express, it is created either by deed, or in writing not by deed, or verbally without writing. When the agency is not express, it may be inferred from the relation of the parties and the nature of the employment without any proof of any express appointment. The agency must be given ahead of time, or subsequently adopted; and in the latter case there must be an act of recognition, or an acquiescence in the act of the agent, from which a recognition may be fairly implied. perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit responsibility of a party for breaches caused by its employees, agents, officials, representatives, or consultants, thereby contradicting agency law principles. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party." The omission weakens the original contract's protection, creating a legal loophole where the principal might avoid liability for the actions of its agents acting within their scope of engagement, which directly contradicts the law's intent to hold principals accountable for their agents' actions.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties acknowledge and understand that all documentation and information , including Confidential Information, provided to the Province of Nova Scotia or HRM, as the case may be, pursuant to this Agreement shall be kept confidential, subject to the provisions of the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act , Province of Nova Scotia, and such other laws of the Province of Nova Scotia and Canada which may apply from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties acknowledge and understand that all documentation and information , including Confidential Information, provided to the Province of Nova Scotia or HRM, as the case may be, pursuant to this Agreement shall be kept confidential, subject to the provisions of the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act , Province of Nova Scotia.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'and such other laws of the Province of Nova Scotia and Canada which may apply from time to time', this significantly weakens the confidentiality protections. This omission limits the scope of applicable laws, potentially violating other federal and provincial laws related to privacy, data protection, and intellectual property. For example, PIPEDA (Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act) in Canada may have stricter requirements for certain types of data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA is a federal law that governs the collection, use, and disclosure of personal information in the course of commercial activities. By omitting the reference to 'other laws,' the agreement might be interpreted as not needing to comply with PIPEDA's requirements if personal information is exchanged, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.justification: The omission of "and such other laws of the Province of Nova Scotia and Canada which may apply from time to time" creates a legal contradiction. The original text acknowledges adherence to all relevant laws, whereas the changed text limits this acknowledgment to the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act of Nova Scotia. This creates a loophole where other relevant federal laws, such as PIPEDA, could be argued as not applicable, directly contradicting the intent to comply with all laws. The scraped snippet regarding PIPEDA highlights that it governs the collection, use, and disclosure of personal information in commercial activities, indicating its relevance. The omission weakens the original obligation to comply with all applicable laws, creating a clear non-compliance issue as it could be interpreted that PIPEDA requirements need not be followed.
